name: Late Latin

ancestors.notes: Greek adstrate: Eastern Empire bilingualism, liturgical vocabulary, phonological influence on aspiration loss (ph→f, th→t, ch→k)

notes: Late Latin (~3rd–6th c. CE). The written Latin of the late Empire and early medieval period, coexisting with diverging spoken Vulgar Latin varieties. Key features: (1) Vowel quantity → quality: the 10→7 vowel merger underway (ĭ/ē→/e/, ĕ→/ɛ/, ŭ/ō→/o/, ŏ→/ɔ/). (2) ae→[ɛ(ː)], oe→[e(ː)] monophthongisation complete. (3) /h/ effectively silent. (4) B/V merger (betacism) visible in misspellings. (5) Palatalisation beginning: /k/ before e,i → [kʲ]; /t/ + i + V → [tsʲ]. (6) Greek aspirated stops → fricatives (ph→[f], th→[t]). Attested: Vulgate, Peregrinatio Aetheriae, Gregory of Tours, Isidore of Seville, Lex Salica. Source: Herman (2000), Väänänen (1981), Adams (2013).

sources.author: Weiss, M.
sources.title: Outline of the Historical and Comparative Grammar of Latin

sources.author: Allen, W.S.
sources.title: Vox Latina: A Guide to the Pronunciation of Classical Latin

sources.author: Clackson, J. & Horrocks, G.
sources.title: The Blackwell History of the Latin Language
